entry.title: Where is data stored maintained in SAP Build Apps (from Visual Cloud Functions or from the App)
entry.content:
Hello

Questions: 

1/ Where is data stored maintained in SAP Build Apps (from Visual Cloud Functions or from the App) ?

Where and how can we choose the DB on which the data should be stored ? How can we easily do the sizing ?

2/ How can that data be accessed (assuming that it is stored on a DB that can identified)? How can that data be extracted ? Is there a SQL/CDS based access mechanism available ? 

3/ Can we develop SAP Build Apps on a HANA database on which we also develop SAP CAP or SAP RAP ? In other words, can we setup a CDS based data model in 1 SAP HANA DB and then develop above it using CAP or RAP or SAP Build Apps/SAP Build Code where the data model is shared ? (I know that this goes against microservices paradigm, but a coherent CDS based data model on which we develop using the appropriate technology seems to me a good compromise ....)  

Context: 

We are considering to use for example SAP Build Apps to enhance C4C V1 in preparation to move in the future to C4C V2. In this context we were wondering where the data of SAP Build Apps is stored ? Also how would that data be enabled for other usages ? Indeed, we might want to extract that data to Data Warehouse, extract back into C4C V1 for other enablement. It is however not at all clear on how that data can be accessed and exploited. For the moment the SAP Build Apps (for sure the backend features VCF) seems to be a huge black box. Making a nice application with low code/no code is marvelous. But if that is generating hidden data that is not accessible, then we have created a new problem. 

PS: It might be that I am thinking wrongly in the cloud context or that I missed something. My apologies if this would be the case.

entry.title: Re: Service Orders/Quotations 'Notes' not available for F...
entry.content: Hi Inês, Would you check with your local consultant or developer whether it is possible to defer your logic execution or implementing a post-processing step? For example, by using an update task to delay your logic execution until after the commit, you ensure that the texts are fully saved in their respective tables before trying to access them. This post-processing step will prevent issues related to trying to read uncommitted or previously saved texts. Following this approach, your custom field updates will reflect the latest data, ensuring the "Notes" are accurately captured and displayed in the output form.

entry.title: Assigning the material name into CT04 characteristic
entry.content:
Hello Dear SAP community,

I couldn't find anything relevant to my issue so I decided to create a new question. I am working in the LO-VC environment with the KMATs that have assigned classes, characteristics and so on -of course there are a functional BOMs created under each KMAT.
I need to forward the KMAT name (material description - table MAKT/ field MAKTX) to one of the classes that are present in the KMAT configuration profile. As far as I know it's possible to create a characteristic that has a reference to name field as shown in picture below, but it is only functioning for manual input of the characteristic value that will be forwarded in the procedure/s.
Is there any way to create the characteristic that will just take over the name/parameter that is present in the system?

Thank You in advance
MN

entry.title: Re: Service Orders/Quotations 'Notes' not available for F...
entry.content: Hi Inês, Would you check with your local consultant or developer whether it is possible to defer your logic execution or implementing a post-processing step?  For example, by using an update task to delay your logic execution until after the commit, you ensure that the texts are fully saved in their respective tables before trying to access them. This post-processing step will prevent issues related to trying to read uncommitted or previously saved texts. Following this approach, your custom field updates will reflect the latest data, ensuring the "Notes" are accurately captured and displayed in the output form.

entry.title: Goods issue with wrong unit cost.
entry.content:
Due to wrong foreign exchange rate, my items in the warehouse were posted by wrong item cost. i.e. the item cost escalated by 1000's.  However, the quantity in either GI or GR are correct. Due to this issue, there has been a significant impact on my GL. 

The issues related to Foreign Currency exchange were corrected, however, I am now stuck on how to correct the values of the GR and GI with wrong unit cost need to be corrected. 

I am not sure if I should be going for inventory revaluation or making the reversal entries wherever possible. But the major concern is that the quantity of goods issue or goods receipt is correct.

 

Thank you everyone in advance. #SAPB1

entry.title: Re: SAP IAS: Attribute from corporate IdP and map with Ro...
entry.content: The attribute that is mapped to a BTP Role Collection can be defined in BTP as you like. Just make sure that the attribut is contained in the Token or SAML Assertion. Of course the default way is to use the groups attribute which can be filled with all the groups a user is assigned to.

entry.title: 8 Steps of EWM Migration Business Suite 9.x to Embedded EWM on S/4 HANA
entry.content:
Hello EWM Folks 

In this blog, we will explore the key prerequisites and steps for migrating from the EWM Business Suite to Embedded EWM on S/4 HANA. EWM is a strategic warehouse management solution from SAP. Since the launch of Embedded EWM with the S/4HANA 1809 release, SAP has been supported their customers running warehouses on Business Suite 9.x in their digital transformation. the ability to simplify their landscape by offering the option to deploy EWM in an embedded 

Choosing between Decentralized EWM and Embedded EWM involves a comprehensive analysis from various perspectives—not just only from functionality- several non-functional criteria must be evaluated, including understanding the business vision, assessing transition risks, considering costs, and evaluating maintainability..ect  While we won't delve into these aspects in this blog, it's crucial to evaluate that as important prerequisites.

Let’s begin with a quick overview and recap of the available deployment options for EWM. This has been discussed in various blogs, but we’ll provide a brief recap here.

 

  1. Embedded EWM on SAP S/4HANA

EWM is tightly integrated on same SAP S/4HANA box, providing a seamless and efficient solution for warehouse management, Offers basic / Advanced warehouse management functionalities, suitable for mid-sized warehouses with standard processes. May have limitations in handling very large or complex high volume warehouses 

     2 .Decentralized EWM on SAP S/4HANA

EWM runs on a separate server platform, providing more flexibility and scalability. Offers advanced warehouse management features, suitable for large-scale warehouses with complex processes. Connects to SAP S/4HANA via RFCs and IDOC .  

Let us also have a look into the license part Embedded EWM : -

in Embedded EWM there are two different licenses based on the functionality that will run , Basic or Advanced EWM 

Basic EWM is  included in the core SAP S/4HANA license. This means that organizations using SAP S/4HANA might not incur additional costs for Basic EWM.

Advanced EWM typically requires a separate license. This means organizations need to purchase additional licenses to access the advanced features and capabilities.

With the Basic Warehouse Management usage mode, you can use the following basic warehousing processes
and functions:

• Inventory Management
• Inbound Processing (for example, Deconsolidation)
• Outbound Processing
• Internal Warehouse Movements
• Physical Inventory
• Reporting

With the Extended Warehouse Management usage mode, you can additionally use the following advanced
warehousing functions:

• Inventory Management Optimization (for example, Slotting)
• Inbound Process Optimization
• Outbound Process Optimization (for example, Waves)
• Material Flow Control
• Yard Management (for example, TU handling, Dock Appointment Scheduling)
• Labor Management
• Value Added Services
• Kitting

Before jump into the major step of EWM Embedded Migration ,Question here what are the major differences between between EWM on business suite and Embedded EWM

 

 

 

Master Data :-

in Embedded EWM No Cor Interface , which mean its not require to Master Data distribution ( Material , Batches , Classifications , Business partners ). EWM Tables /SAPAPO/MATMAP , /SAPAPO/MATKEY,   /SAPAPO/MATTXT ,  /SAPAPO/MARM , /SAPAPO/MEANGTIN , /SCMB/MATID_MAP , /SAPAPO/MATEXEC its read S/4 HANA tables directly MARA , MAKT , MARM , MEAN .

However, there are certain warehouse-dependent tables that require manual maintenance for data such as /SAPAPO/MATAPN.

DDIC Objects & Tables :-

Embedded EWM eliminates the need for redundant configuration tables, as the following tables become cross-functional across all S/4HANA applications, including EWM:

 

Catch-weight tolerance group

CW profile for CW quantities

Delivery priority

Handling indicator

Handling unit type

Incoterms

Packing group

Quality inspection group

Serial number profile

Shipping conditions

Transportation group

Warehouse material group

Warehouse storage condition

Transaction Data Distribution : -

Despite QRFC remaining an option for delivery-based integration between S/4HANA and EWM, synchronized goods movements offer an alternative that does not require document distribution. Supported scenarios for goods receipt and goods issue processes can be posted using MIGO. For more information about synchronized goods movements, please refer to this link 

Functionality : -

When deciding on the product version for a new EWM implementation project, SAP recommends carefully reviewing the differences between decentralized EWM on SAP S/4HANA Release XXXX and SAP EWM 9.x.

If you refer to the usual SAP release notes regarding information and restrictions for decentralized EWM on SAP S/4HANA, you will find an important attachment. The note and its attached document, "EWM Deployment Differences," outline the feature parity gaps between decentralized EWM on S/4HANA and SAP EWM 9.5. Additionally, decentralized EWM on S/4HANA includes new features that are not available in SAP EWM 9.5. SAP has published these new features for each S/4HANA release in the "What's New Viewer - SAP S/4HANA," which is accessible through the SAP Help Portal. All SAP S/4HANA versions after 1709 offer more functionality than SAP EWM 9.5.

Also Its important also to check the compatibility of existing Add-on whether that's compatible with S/4 or not For a list of supported add-ons, see SAP Note 2214409. For information about uninstalling add-ons, see SAP Note 2011192. 

Innovation : -

Embedded analytics was not available on business suite , but know customers able to access and analyze real-time data directly within the EWM system, without the need for separate reporting tools. This provides warehouse managers and other stakeholders with a comprehensive overview of  their KPIs and operational metrics , 

Warehouse insight  is an SAP Cloud based tool that leverages advanced algorithms and machine learning to optimize warehouse operations and resource utilization. It provides real-time insights, KPI analysis, and simulation capabilities to enhance warehouse efficiency.

 

8 Steps Migration procedure : -

 

 

Step #1 System Setup

In this step its contain basic sittings system for EWM in SAP S/4HANA. from the following aspects of the configuration:
Define logical system, Business system, System Configuration of qRFC communication between SAP EWM and other SAP S/4HANA applications ,Technical Prerequisites for qRFC Communication Creating ,Dummy Logical System Manually .

Definition of a warehouse and integration of the warehouse in the enterprise structure Configuration, and complete configuration relevant , align number ranges from HU and Business Partners 

Step #2 Custom Code 

By using SE03 Transport Organizer you can transport custom code from the source to the target system , then Run ATC check or perform Custom Code Impact Analysis for the custom codes to identify necessary codes to be changed.

Step #3 Manage and compare common 13 Customize table

If you remember in this blog there will be 13 customizing table shared across different application , Compare the Business Suite EWM table content with the ERP table entries and adjust/create missing entries in ERP.

Step #4 Manage other Local Customizing

In this step perform Customizing transport for existing EWM confirmation and activate BC-Sets

Step #5 Role & Authorization

By using TCODE PFCG .It is possible to put all roles in the source system into a transport  and import it transport into the target system S/4HANA

Step #6 Migrate Local Master Data

Since Migration of different master data become available in different ways whether using staging table or Direct Transfer in Migration cockpit  With SAP S/4HANA 2020 10 more objects were introduced and with 2021 and 2022 again a few new objects. From SAP S/4HANA 2022 on, all these migrations objects are also supported in EWM embedded in S/4 HANA.However there are local master data a part from standard migration which need to be investigated , like if the master data has custom fields , is these are client or warehouse specific or not ..ect to define the way of migrating these data .

Step #7 Migrate Stock 

There are different ways to Migrate stocks whether by using Direct transfer which enables you to migrate warehouse stock data from the source EWM system to the target SAP S/4HANA system, based on the default selection criteria set for the migration object. This migration technique posts data to the target system using a function module.or using initial stock upload /SCWM/ISU

Step #8 Close Document  

All transactional documents need to be completed or closed before migration .

entry.title: Top Cypress Testing Errors and How to Avoid Them
entry.content:
The first one that will be described is the error related to cy.clearCokies().

This kind of error typically occurs in the context of Cypress, which is a JavaScript-based end-to-end testing framework. Cypress is used for testing web applications, and cy.clearCookies() is a command that clears all the cookies that are stored for the current domain. 

Possible Causes of cy.clearCookies() Error: 

Context or Domain Issues: The error may arise if Cypress attempts to clear cookies outside the context of the domain being tested. cy.clearCookies() is domain-specific, meaning it can only clear cookies that are set for the domain the test is currently running on. If there is a domain mismatch or if the test is not currently interacting with a domain, the command might fail. 

Misconfigured Cypress Settings: Sometimes, the error might be due to incorrect or missing configuration settings in your Cypress tests, such as not specifying the right base URL (Universal Resource Locators) or domain, which can confuse the command about which cookies to clear. 

Third-Party Cookies: Clearing third-party cookies can also cause issues, especially if the cookies are not accessible due to cross-origin restrictions. If your test interacts with multiple domains or subdomains, you might need to manage cookies more explicitly. 

Timing Issues: The error might occur if cy.clearCookies() is called at an inappropriate time during the test, such as before the application fully loads or before any cookies have been set. 

Browser-Specific Behaviour: Different browsers might handle cookies differently, especially when it comes to privacy settings or cross-origin policies. This can lead to inconsistent behaviour when clearing cookies. 

Debugging Steps: 

Check Domain: Ensure that the test is running on the expected domain when cy.clearCookies() is called. 

Use cy.clearCookies({ domain: "yourdomain.com" }): Explicitly specify the domain to avoid domain mismatch issues. 

Check Timing: Make sure cy.clearCookies() is called after the page has loaded and cookies have been set. 

Browser Configuration: Verify that your test settings (like browser settings) are not interfering with cookie handling. 

Look at Cypress Logs: Check the Cypress logs to see if there is any additional information about why the error occurred. 

If the problem persists, reviewing the specific error message in the Cypress console can provide more insights into what might be going wrong. 

 

The second type of error that often occurs is related to the cy.click() command. 

This kind of error happens because the element you are trying to click on is being covered by another element. 

Breakdown of the Error: 

Error Message: "Timed out retrying after 30050ms: cy.click() failed because this element is being covered by another element." 

Specific Element: The element in question is an SVG icon with the following attributes: 
html 
Copy code 
<svg class="MuiSvgIcon-root MuiSvgIcon-colorSuccess MuiSvgIcon-fontSizeMedium css-nm8jzx" ...> 

Issue: The error indicates that Cypress tried to click on the specified SVG element, but it was unable to do so because another element (in this case, an HTML element) is covering it. 

Common Causes: 

Overlapping Elements: Another element (like a modal, loading spinner or an invisible overlay) might be positioned on top of the element you are trying to interact with. 

Timing Issues: The page might not be fully loaded or an animation might still be in progress, causing the element to be temporarily covered when Cypress attempts to click on it. 

Incorrect Element Detection: Sometimes, Cypress might be trying to click on a different element than intended, due to similar classes or IDs. 

Solutions: 

Wait for the Element to be Uncovered: Use cy.wait() or wait for a specific condition to ensure the element is not covered when Cypress attempts to click on it. 

Example: 
javascript 
Copy code 
cy.get('your-element-selector').should('be.visible').click(); 

Scroll into View: If the element is off-screen or partially hidden, you can scroll it into view before clicking. 

Example: 
javascript 
Copy code 
cy.get('your-element-selector').scrollIntoView().click(); 

Force Click: As a last resort, you can bypass the error by using {force: true} in your cy.click() command. This forces the click even if the element is covered. 

Example: 
javascript 
Copy code 
cy.get('your-element-selector').click({ force: true }); 

Caution: While this can solve the problem, it is better to resolve the underlying issue (like an element being covered) rather than forcing a click. 

Check for Overlapping Elements: Ensure there are no overlapping elements like popups, modals or invisible divs that could interfere with the click. 

By following these steps, you should be able to resolve the error and ensure Cypress interacts with the correct element during your tests. 

In summary it is crucial to name the most potential causes of the described errors and to get to know the solution, what can prevent the errors appearance in the first place.

entry.title: Re: ATP check for sto purchase orders
entry.content:
Hi Zareena,

In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition you can find the setting for STO in the SSCUIs. For this, open the app "Manage Your Solution" and navigate to the SSCUI with the name 'Configure Delivery Type & Availability Check Procedure by Plant' (its SSCUI ID is 103424). Do do this, you can filter for Application Area 'Sourcing and Procurement' and Item 'Purchase Order Processing'.

You can find the so-called Checking Rule maintained. By standard content it is 'A'. This translates to the same ATP Scope of Check settings shown in your screenshot. 

Regards,
Andreas

entry.title: How to replace BW/HANA Composite Provider with new Source Object
entry.content:
Hello All,

In HANA calculation view , we can replace source table X with the some other Table Y easily by using the Option "Replace with DataSource" & in next pop-up/screen it gives me the flexibility to map the columns b/w old table X & the new table Y.

This way no further adjustments required in that CV if we could map all of the required columns.

The same option , I am trying at HANA Composite Provider to replace one source with the new one using the below "replace" option.. But its not giving any option to map the columns b/w old vs new sources. It simply removing the entire source & replacing it with the new one. All of the existing mapping from that Old source are removed.

Is this the expected behavior w.r.t HANA HCPR ? if yes , how can we handle this scenarios in a better way without loosing the existing mappings from the old source.

Thanks

Kiran K

entry.title: Re: Query to list components and unit price
entry.content:
Hello Rah,

you must use the following link to the ITT1 table:

LEFT JOIN ITT1 T2 ON T1.[ItemCode] = T2.[Code] AND T0.ItemCode = T2.Father

But since you said that "there are multiple phantom part numbers in the BOM", then a clear link to the ITT1 table is no longer possible. That won't work.

Best regards

Inga

entry.title: SAP Discovery Center at SAP TechEd 2024: Sessions, Resources, and Highlights
entry.content:
Planning, preparations, and excitement are all in full swing for SAP TechEd 2024! The event will kick-off virtually before heading out on a global tour, making attendance easier than ever. The session catalog is filled with hot topics and content experts, eager to share the latest innovations and news coming out of SAP. If you haven’t done so already, register for SAP TechEd 2024.

Whether you are new to SAP Business Technology Platform or a returning veteran, SAP Discovery Center is your go-to resource. From detailed use cases and service information to step-by-step missions and reference architectures, the Discovery Center helps you realize the full potential of SAP BTP, accelerating product adoption and driving informed decisions. Haven’t explored it yet? Now’s the perfect time! Go to SAP Discovery Center.

TechEd Sessions Featuring SAP Discovery Center

For this year’s SAP TechEd, Discovery Center will be featured, including deep dive demos, in the following sessions:

Session XP180 | Get started with SAP BTP: Discover and accelerate with SAP Discovery Center
Explore the latest enhancements in speed of project implementation and personalization in SAP Discovery Center. See the powerful advantages of guided implementation projects and reference architectures curated by SAP. Learn how to effectively use the service catalog in SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), the extensive list of real-world use cases, and your control center. Whether a new or experienced user, you’ll get the knowledge and tools to accelerate your SAP BTP journey.
Speakers: Nis Boy Naeve and Marco Geldmacher

Session XP105 | Start your journey with SAP BTP and confidently accelerate adoption
Are you new to SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) or seeking guidance on best practices and architectural decisions? Take a look at the powerful advantages of guided implementation projects curated by SAP experts and partners with SAP Discovery Center. Discover how the SAP BTP Guidance Framework assists you with decision guides, reference architectures, and best practices to design, develop, and operate enterprise-grade applications on SAP BTP.
Speakers: Konstantin Heine and Cecily Sorenson

Session XP204 | Modernize your technology landscape with SAP BTP and SAP LeanIX solutions
Discover how SAP LeanIX solutions can transform your as-is technology landscape leveraging SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). Learn how to get instant visibility in your as-is SAP landscape using automated application discovery. Model your SAP BTP landscape with best practices, define your to-be technology landscape with the help of reference content based on SAP BTP Guidance Framework, and manage your technology transformation, governance compliance, and risks using report templates.
Speakers: Katrin von Ahsen and Jens Rieskamp

Don’t Miss the SAP TechEd Learning Zone

It wouldn’t be TechEd without a core emphasis on learning and upskilling opportunities. This year’s TechEd Learning Zone features tailored learning experiences for each content track. Within each track you will also find reference to six key use cases. These use cases have been hand-selected for their topic relevance and quality guidance.

More About SAP BTP Use Cases

SAP BTP use cases consist of customer-proven, well-documented missions for an easy implementation of SAP BTP. They support you in solving problems in a repeatable manner to achieve the desired business outcome faster. To learn more about what SAP BTP looks like in real life and how it drives business innovation and success, explore the featured use cases.

What’s New in SAP Discovery Center

Exciting new features are coming to SAP Discovery Center! Stay tuned by subscribing to this blog post or following me on LinkedIn to be one of the first to know—you won’t want to miss it!

entry.title: Re: Posting Material Master via OData Service
entry.content:
Hello Varun,

 

Material Tax Classification is relevant for sales data, and if you are maintaining the sales view it is required.

So, please check what you have configured in Tax Classification and provide the data accordingly in correct place to create Material Master record.

 

thanks,

Ankur

entry.title: How to Map Fields from Recruitment Management to Onboarding in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
When integrating Recruitment Management with Onboarding 1.0 in SAP SuccessFactors, it's crucial to map the required fields properly. Here's a simplified step-by-step guide to achieve that: 

Step 1: Identify Required Fields 

First, understand the client’s requirements and identify the fields that need to be transferred from Recruitment Management to Onboarding. These fields can be created within specific templates in Recruitment Management, such as: 

  • Job Requisition 
  • Job Application 
  • Job Offer 

Fields cannot be created in the Candidate Profile, as it is not mapped in the Onboarding process.

Step 2: Create the Fields in Recruitment Management 

Once you know which fields are needed, create them within the respective template in Recruitment Management. You can choose the correct template (e.g., Job Requisition) based on where they are required for onboarding. 

Step 3: Access Onboarding 1.0 Integration Setup 

To begin mapping the fields, search for Setup Onboarding 1.0 Integration in the search bar of SAP SuccessFactors. Once inside the setup page, navigate to Settings and choose the template in which the fields were created.

Step 4: Map Fields from Recruitment to Onboarding 

In the selected template, you’ll see a screen with two sections: 

  • Left side: Onboarding fields 
  • Right side: Recruiting Management fields 

Here, you can map the fields by selecting the corresponding fields from the dropdown menus. Fields created in Recruitment Management will be available on the right-hand side, and you can link them to the Onboarding fields on the left.

 

Conclusion: 

Following these steps will allow you to map fields seamlessly from Recruitment Management to Onboarding 1.0, ensuring that client requirements are met efficiently. This streamlined process helps avoid issues during onboarding and ensures data continuity.

entry.title: Extending the SAP CCO Configuration Menu: A Detailed Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

The ability to extend and customize SAP systems through plugins provides companies with a powerful tool to efficiently address specific requirements. In this guide, I will walk you through the development of the Additional Tab in Config plugin, which adds an extra tab to the SAP-CCO configuration interface. This tab is designed to display and manage SAP partner data. The approach outlined here can also be applied to many other use cases where an additional tab in this interface is needed, such as configuring a more complex plugin.

We will go through each step in detail, explain the techniques used, and provide comprehensive code documentation so you can fully understand the concepts and apply them directly in your project.

This guide is suitable for both developers new to the SAP environment and experienced developers alike.

If you don't have any prior experience with CCO plugin development, I recommend exploring additional resources like sap-customer-checkout-plugin-development  to gain deeper insights into the topic of plugin development.

1. The MainClass: Central Control of the Plugin

The MainClass is the core of the plugin. Here, you define the fundamental properties of the plugin and implement key functionalities such as JavaScript injection and HTTP request handling.

Step 1.1: Creating the MainClass

Start by creating a new Java class named MainClass that extends BasePlugin. This class will be the central hub for all core functions of your plugin.

public class MainClass extends BasePlugin {

    // Method to return the unique plugin ID
    @Override
    public String getId() {
        return PluginPropertiesConstants.PLUGIN_ID;
    }

    // Method to return the name of the plugin
    @Override
    public String getName() {
        return PluginPropertiesConstants.PLUGIN_NAME;
    }

    // Method to return the version of the plugin
    @Override
    public String getVersion() {
        return getClass().getPackage().getImplementationVersion();
    }
    
    // Method to initialize the plugin
    @Override
    public void startup() {
        super.startup();
        LOG.info("Additional Tab in Config Plugin started.");
    }
}

Explanation:

  • Inheritance from BasePlugin: By inheriting from BasePlugin, your plugin seamlessly integrates with the SAP plugin infrastructure.
  • Providing Metadata: The methods getId, getName, and getVersion provide essential information about your plugin.

Step 1.2: Injecting JavaScript into SAP Screens

JavaScript injection allows you to embed custom scripts into specific SAP screens, enabling dynamic UI enhancements or the provision of additional functionality.

@JSInject(targetScreen = "NGUI")
public InputStream[] jsInject() {strong text
    // Injecting JavaScript into the NGUI screen
    return new InputStream[]{
        getClass().getClassLoader().getResourceAsStream("ngui.js")
    };
}

@JSInject(targetScreen = "configuration")
public InputStream[] jsInjectConfiguration() {
    // Injecting JavaScript into the configuration screen
    return new InputStream[]{
        getClass().getClassLoader().getResourceAsStream("pluginConfiguration.js")
    };
}

Explanation:

  • @JSInject Annotation: This annotation allows you to inject JavaScript code into specific SAP screens.
  • Returning an InputStream: The method returns an InputStream that contains the JavaScript to be embedded into the respective screen.

2. JavaScript Integration: Extending the User Interface

Now we have the ability to inject custom JS code, and we will create it now.

Step 2.1: Creating the JavaScript File

First, create a new JavaScript file that contains the code to extend the user interface. This file will be injected into the SAP system to dynamically manipulate the UI.

Step 2.2: Preparing for Dynamic Tab Creation

Use jQuery's ready() function to ensure the script runs once the document is fully loaded.

$(document).ready(function () {
    // Logic for creating a new tab will go here
});

Step 2.3: Defining the Tab Header

Define the HTML structure for the new tab header.

const tabHeader = `
    <li role="tab" tabindex="-1" class="ui-tabs-tab ui-corner-top ui-state-default ui-tab" 
        aria-controls="configuration_tabs-rfconfig" aria-labelledby="ui-id-18" 
        aria-selected="false" aria-expanded="false">
        <a href="#configuration_tabs-rfconfig" tabindex="-1" class="ui-tabs-anchor" id="ui-id-18">SAP Partner Contact</a>
    </li>`;

Step 2.4: Appending the Tab Header to the UI

Now append the tabHeader to the tab navigation menu in the UI.

$('#configuration_tabs .ui-tabs-nav').append(tabHeader);

 

Step 2.5: Creating the Tab Content Structure

Define the HTML structure for the tab content.

const tabContent = `
    <div id="configuration_tabs-rfconfig" class="configuration_tab ui-tabs-panel ui-corner-bottom ui-widget-content" 
         aria-labelledby="ui-id-18" role="tabpanel" aria-hidden="true" style="display: none;">
        <form id="customerForm">
            <div class="form-group">
                <label for="companyName">Company Name:</label>
                <input type="text" id="companyName" name="companyName" value="" disabled>
            </div>
            <div class="form-group">
                <label for="address">Address:</label>
                <input type="text" id="address" name="address" value="" disabled>
            </div>
            <div class="form-group">
                <label for="city">City:</label>
                <input type="text" id="city" name="city" value="" disabled>
            </div>
            <!-- More form fields for postalCode, phone, fax, email, etc. -->
            <div class="form-group">
                <button type="button" id="editBtn">Edit</button>
                <button type="submit" id="saveBtn" disabled>Save</button>
            </div>
        </form>
    </div>`;

Step 2.6: Appending the Tab Content to the UI

Append the tabContent to the configuration tab container in the UI.

$('#configuration_tabs').append(tabContent);

Step 2.7: Populating the Tab with Backend Data

Use the following function to populate the form fields with data from the backend.

function addNewTab(data) {
    let companyName = data.companyName || "Unknown";
    let address = data.address || "Unknown";
    let city = data.city || "Unknown";
    let postalCode = data.postalCode || "Unknown";
    let phone = data.phone || "Unknown";
    let fax = data.fax || "Unknown";
    let email = data.email || "Unknown";
    let management = data.management || "Unknown";
    let legalForm = data.legalForm || "Unknown";
    let court = data.court || "Unknown";
    let registrationNumber = data.registrationNumber || "Unknown";
    let vatId = data.vatId || "Unknown";

    $('#companyName').val(companyName);
    $('#address').val(address);
    $('#city').val(city);
    $('#postalCode').val(postalCode);
    $('#phone').val(phone);
    $('#fax').val(fax);
    $('#email').val(email);
    $('#management').val(management);
    $('#legalForm').val(legalForm);
    $('#court').val(court);
    $('#registrationNumber').val(registrationNumber);
    $('#vatId').val(vatId);
}

Step 2.8: Triggering the Tab Creation with Backend Data

Finally, use AJAX to retrieve the backend data and pass it to the addNewTab function.

function fetchDataAndAddTab() {
    $.ajax({
        url: 'PluginServlet', 
        type: 'GET',
        success: function (response) {
            addNewTab(response);
        },
        error: function (xhr, status, error) {
            alert("An error occurred while fetching the data: " + error);
        }
    });
}

3. Handling HTTP Requests

Now that the user interface is fully set up and the user can modify data, the next crucial step is connecting these changes to the backend.

3.1 PluginServletHandler: Central Request Processing

@ListenToExit(exitName = "PluginServlet.callback.post")
public void vicallbackHandlerPost(Object caller, Object[] args) {
    try {
        HttpServletRequest request = (HttpServletRequest) args[0];
        HttpServletResponse response = (HttpServletResponse) args[1];
        PluginServletHandler.handleRequest(request, response);
    } catch (IOException e) {
        LOG.error("Error handling POST request: {}", e.getMessage(), e);
    }
}

@ListenToExit(exitName = "PluginServlet.callback.get")
public void vicallbackHandlerGet(Object caller, Object[] args) {
    try {
        HttpServletRequest request = (HttpServletRequest) args[0];
        HttpServletResponse response = (HttpServletResponse) args[1];
        PluginServletHandler.handleRequest(request, response);
    } catch (IOException e) {
        LOG.error("Error handling GET request: {}", e.getMessage(), e);
    }
}

The PluginServletHandler is a crucial component of your plugin, responsible for handling HTTP requests. Here, you separate the logic for GET and POST requests and ensure that these requests are processed correctly.

Step 3.2: Creating the PluginServletHandler Class

Start by creating a final class PluginServletHandler that serves as the central point for processing HTTP requests.

public final class PluginServletHandler {

    private PluginServletHandler() {
        // Prevents instantiation of the class
    }

    public static void handleRequest(HttpServletRequest request, HttpServletResponse response) throws IOException {
        String method = request.getMethod();
        if (StringUtils.equalsIgnoreCase(method, "POST")) {
            // Process POST request (save data)
            processPostRequest(request, response);
        } else if (StringUtils.equalsIgnoreCase(method, "GET")) {
            // Process GET request (retrieve data)
            processGetRequest(response);
        } else {
            response.getWriter().write("{\"error\":\"Unsupported request method\"}");
            response.setStatus(HttpServletResponse.SC_METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED);
        }
    }
}

Explanation:

  • Static handleRequest Method: This method is the central point where all HTTP requests are processed.
  • Preventing Instantiation: The private constructor ensures that the class cannot be instantiated.

Step 3.3: Processing POST Requests

Processing POST requests is essential for receiving data from the frontend and storing it in the database.

private static void processPostRequest(HttpServletRequest request, HttpServletResponse response) throws IOException {
    response.setContentType("application/json");
    response.setCharacterEncoding("UTF-8");

    ObjectMapper objectMapper = new ObjectMapper();
    PartnerDataDTO customerData = objectMapper.readValue(request.getReader(), PartnerDataDTO.class);

    PartnerDataDAO dao = PartnerDataDAO.INSTANCE;

    try (CDBSession dbSession = CDBSessionFactory.instance.createSession()) {
        dao.createTable();
        dbSession.beginTransaction();
        dao.upsert(customerData);
        dbSession.commitTransaction();

        response.getWriter().write("{\"status\":\"success\"}");
        response.setStatus(HttpServletResponse.SC_OK);
    } catch (Exception e) {
        LOG.error("Failed to save data", e);
        response.getWriter().write("{\"error\":\"Failed to save data: " + e.getMessage() + "\"}");
        response.setStatus(HttpServletResponse.SC_INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR);
    }
}

Explanation:

  • JSON Processing: The processPostRequest method reads the incoming data as JSON and converts it into a PartnerDataDTO object.
  • Database Operations: The table is created if it does not exist, and the data is either inserted or updated.

Step 3.4: Processing GET Requests

GET requests are typically used to retrieve data from the backend and return it to the frontend.

private static void processGetRequest(HttpServletResponse response) throws IOException {
    response.setContentType("application/json");
    response.setCharacterEncoding("UTF-8");

    PartnerDataDAO dao = PartnerDataDAO.INSTANCE;

    try (CDBSession dbSession = CDBSessionFactory.instance.createSession()) {
        PartnerDataDTO customerData = dao.getPartnerData(dbSession);
        ObjectMapper objectMapper = new ObjectMapper();
        String jsonResponse = objectMapper.writeValueAsString(customerData);

        response.getWriter().write(jsonResponse);
        response.setStatus(HttpServletResponse.SC_OK);
    } catch (Exception e) {
        LOG.error("Failed to retrieve data", e);
        response.getWriter().write("{\"error\":\"Failed to retrieve data: " + e.getMessage() + "\"}");
        response.setStatus(HttpServletResponse.SC_INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR);
    }
}

Explanation:

  • Data Retrieval: The processGetRequest method uses the PartnerDataDAO to retrieve the necessary data from the database.

4. PartnerDataDAO: Data Access Layer

The PartnerDataDAO manages access to the database and implements methods for creating the necessary tables, as well as for inserting and updating records.

Step 4.1: Creating the PartnerDataDAO Class

Create an enum PartnerDataDAO that functions as a Singleton and encapsulates database access.

public enum PartnerDataDAO {
    INSTANCE;

    private static final String TABLE_NAME = "HOK_CONFIG_PARTNER_DATA";

    private static final String QUERY_CREATE_TABLE = String.format(
        "CREATE TABLE %s ( " +
        "ID INTEGER NOT NULL GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY (START WITH 1, INCREMENT BY 1) , PRIMARY KEY (ID), " +
        "COMPANY_NAME VARCHAR(255), " +
        "ADDRESS VARCHAR(255), " +
        "CITY VARCHAR(255), " +
        "POSTAL_CODE VARCHAR(255), " +
        "PHONE VARCHAR(255), " +
        "FAX VARCHAR(255), " +
        "EMAIL VARCHAR(255), " +
        "MANAGEMENT VARCHAR(255), " +
        "LEGAL_FORM VARCHAR(255), " +
        "COURT VARCHAR(255), " +
        "REGISTRATION_NUMBER VARCHAR(255), " +
        "VAT_ID VARCHAR(255))", TABLE_NAME);

Explanation:

  • Singleton Pattern: By using an enum as a Singleton, you ensure that there is only one instance of PartnerDataDAO responsible for database access.

Step 4.2: Table Management

Implement methods for creating and managing database tables.

public void createTable() {
    executeUpdate(QUERY_CREATE_TABLE, "Table %s created", TABLE_NAME);
    createIndex("IDX_COMPANY_NAME", TABLE_NAME, "COMPANY_NAME");
    createIndex("IDX_ADDRESS", TABLE_NAME, "ADDRESS");
    createIndex("IDX_CITY", TABLE_NAME, "CITY");
    createIndex("IDX_POSTAL_CODE", TABLE_NAME, "POSTAL_CODE");
}

public void createIndex(String indexName, String tableName, String... columns) {
    String query = String.format("CREATE INDEX %s ON %s (%s)", indexName, tableName, String.join(",", columns));
    executeUpdate(query, "Index %s created", indexName);
}

Explanation:

  • Table and Index Creation: The createTable method creates the necessary database table if it does not exist.

Step 4.3: Inserting and Updating Data

Implement the upsert method to either insert or update data as needed.

public void upsert(PartnerDataDTO customerDTO) {
    try (CDBSession dbSession = CDBSessionFactory.instance.createSession()) {
        dbSession.beginTransaction();
        long count = ((Number) dbSession.getEM().createNativeQuery("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM " + TABLE_NAME).getSingleResult()).longValue();

        if (count > 0) {
            updateEntry(customerDTO, dbSession);
            LOG.info("Entry in table {} updated", TABLE_NAME);
        } else {
            insert(customerDTO, dbSession);
            LOG.info("New entry inserted into table {}", TABLE_NAME);
        }

        dbSession.commitTransaction();
    } catch (Exception e) {
        LOG.error("Could not perform upsert operation on table {}: {}", TABLE_NAME, e.getMessage(), e);
    }
}

Explanation:

  • upsert Method: This method checks if an entry exists in the table. If so, the entry is updated; otherwise, a new entry is created. This ensures that there is always only one entry in the database, which makes sense for this example. However, in other use cases, it might be necessary to create a new entry each time.

Step 4.4: Retrieving Data

Implement a method to retrieve data from the database.

public PartnerDataDTO getPartnerData(CDBSession dbSession) {
    String selectQuery = String.format("SELECT * FROM %s", TABLE_NAME);
    try {
        Query query = dbSession.getEM().createNativeQuery(selectQuery);
        List<Object[]> resultList = query.getResultList();
        if (!resultList.isEmpty()) {
            return map(resultList).get(0);
        }
    } catch (Exception e) {
        LOG.error("Could not retrieve customer data from table {}: {}", TABLE_NAME, e.getMessage(), e);
    }
    return null;
}

private List map(List<Object[]> resultList) {
    List entries = new ArrayList<>();
    for (Object[] data : resultList) {
        PartnerDataDTO dto = new PartnerDataDTO();
        dto.setCompanyName((String) data[1]);
        dto.setAddress((String) data[2]);
        dto.setCity((String) data[3]);
        dto.setPostalCode((String) data[4]);
        dto.setPhone((String) data[5]);
        dto.setFax((String) data[6]);
        dto.setEmail((String) data[7]);
        dto.setManagement((String) data[8]);
        dto.setLegalForm((String) data[9]);
        dto.setCourt((String) data[10]);
        dto.setRegistrationNumber((String) data[11]);
        dto.setVatId((String) data[12]);
        entries.add(dto);
    }
    return entries;
}

Explanation:

  • Data Query: The getPartnerData method retrieves data from the table and returns it as a PartnerDataDTO.

5. PartnerDataDTO: Data Transfer Object

The PartnerDataDTO serves as a container for the data that is transferred between the various layers of your application.

Step 5.1: Creating the PartnerDataDTO Class

Create a simple Java class PartnerDataDTO that contains all relevant data fields and their corresponding getter and setter methods.

public class PartnerDataDTO {
    private String companyName;
    private String address;
    private String city;
    private String postalCode;
    private String phone;
    private String fax;
    private String email;
    private String management;
    private String legalForm;
    private String court;
    private String registrationNumber;
    private String vatId;

    // Getter and setter methods for each field
    public String getCompanyName() {
        return companyName;
    }

    public void setCompanyName(String companyName) {
        this.companyName = companyName;
    }

    // Other getter and setter methods for the remaining fields...
}

Explanation:

  • DTO Pattern: The PartnerDataDTO serves as a Data Transfer Object.

6. Conclusion

In this guide, we walked through each step of creating an SAP plugin that extends the SAP-CCO configuration interface. With a clear structure and the application of modern web technologies, we developed a robust solution that is both flexible and user-friendly. The techniques and examples presented here provide a solid foundation for developing your own plugins to fully leverage your SAP environment.

entry.title: IBGI-Invoice before goods issue customizing on SAP EWM
entry.content:
Blog porpose:

As a consultant, I was tasked with implementing IBGI in SAP EWM, but found a lack of comprehensive guides detailing the entire process. Therefore, I decided to create this blog to thoroughly explain all the customizing steps involved, accompanied by screenshots from SAP EWM.

In this blog, I also emphasize the importance of having HU updates in ERP before launching IBGI and explain how to do it.

What is IBGI:

IBGI is a process within EWM to initiate the invoicing process before issuing the goods. It is typically used to start the customs procedures, which can take time to complete.

It is not possible to perform an IBGI before completing Picking and creating the outbound delivery.

The sequence is as follows: ODO → Picking → OD → IBGI → Loading → PGI

We can initiate the IBGI process for a single delivery or multiple deliveries.

Step-by-Step Guide for SAP EWM IBGI activation:

The IBGI should be activited per Sales document type and sales organization, using the customizing below:

Logistic execution→Extended warehouse management intergation→Billing settings→Determine Invoice before Goods issue.

Allow the invoice before goods issue in the Outbound Delivery Document Type.

Extended warehouse management→good issue Process→outbound delivery→Define document types for outbound delivery process

Make sure that the invoice before goods issue is allowed for the outbound delivery Item:

Extended warehouse management→good issue Process→outbound delivery→Define Item types for Outbound delivery process

This setting is fundamental for the IBGI process.

By activating 'HU Update,' we allow the system to create the packing Handling Unit (HU) in the ERP system before goods issue through the PPF action: /SCWM/MSG_PRD_SEND.

Extended warehouse management→Cross process setting →Delivery warehouse request→Process management and control

In order to print the invoice, assign the print profil of IBGI to local prtinter and make the release as Immed:

Logistic exécution→Extended warehouse management intergation→Billing settings→Assign Output types and printer to print Profiles

Assign the same print profil to the invoice application V3:

Logistic execution→Transportation→Output control→Define print profiles

Add the condition type REA0 to the relevante procedure with the requirement 417:

Logistic execution →shipping→Output control→maintaine output determination for outbound deliveries → maintaine output determination procedure 

Make sure that the Route in your Outbound delivery is maintained on the transaction /SCWM/IBGI

In this transaction, we must choose whether we want to group deliveries on the same invoice or not

By following these steps, you can customize the IBGI process to meet your business requirements.

Thank you.

 

Mohamed MALYH

entry.title: EWM Queue error “No document type found for delivery EL from system”
entry.content:
Outbound delivery:

Outbound delivery is a document that represents the process of shipping goods from the warehouse to a customer or another location. It is a crucial component of the order fulfillment process, detailing the items to be shipped, their quantities, and their destinations.

Warehouse Task:

It is a fundamental element of warehouse operations that represents a specific action or set of actions to be performed within the warehouse. Warehouse tasks are used to manage and direct various warehouse activities, such as picking, putaway, and inventory handling.

Document type:

Document type is a classification used to define and manage different types of documents or transactions within the warehouse management system. Document types help in organizing and controlling various warehouse processes by specifying how different types of documents should be processed, managed, and handled.

Delivery Type:

It is a classification used to define and manage different types of outbound deliveries within the warehouse management system. Delivery types determine how specific types of delivery documents are processed and handled, including the rules and procedures associated with each delivery type.

Stock Transfer Order:

STO refers to the process of moving inventory from one location to another within or between warehouses. Stock transfers are essential for managing inventory, optimizing warehouse operations, and ensuring that stock levels are appropriately adjusted based on storage needs and operational requirements.

Return Delivery:

It is a type of outbound delivery used to process the return of goods from a customer or another location back to the warehouse. This process is crucial for managing returns, handling reverse logistics, and maintaining accurate inventory records.

Post Goods Issue:

It is an essential process that represents the physical removal of goods from the warehouse inventory. It is a crucial step in various warehouse and logistics processes, including outbound deliveries, internal movements, and inventory management. The goods issue process updates inventory records to reflect the fact that items have been dispatched or otherwise removed from storage.

Issue:

The EWM Queue error “No document type found for delivery EL from system” is caused because of the missing configuration document type ZS for the Delivery type EL is missing.

 

Step1: Create the STO (Stock Transfer order) using the transaction code ME21N

 

 

 

Step2: Create the Outbound delivery using the transaction code VL10B

 

 

 
Navigate to the Purchase order tab, provide the Purchasing document number and then Click on the execute button.

 

 

The Outbound delivery will be created and distributed to EWM

 

 

Step3:

Navigate to the outbound delivery using the transaction code /N/SCWM/PRDO

Confirming the warehouse order.

 

Performed Post Goods Issue

 

Step4:

Inbound delivery is automatically created as soon as the PGI is completed for the outbound delivery.

 

 

Step5:

Inbound delivery is stuck in queue and not processed to EWM with EWM queue error “No document type found for delivery type EL from system”

 

Step6:

Maintained the configuration document type ZS for the Delivery type EL is missing in the EWM system.

 

Step7:

Reprocessed the Inbound queue using the transaction code.

 

 

Step8:

The Inbound delivery is distributed to EWM.

Navigate to the transaction code /N/SCWM/PRDI to display the Inbound delivery.

 

 

Conclusion:

Solution for the EWM Queue error “No document type found for delivery EL from system” is provided.

entry.title: How to read a CSV file from SFTP Server location
entry.content:
In this blog, I am documenting how I read a csv file from SFTP server, convert the data into XML format using CSV to XML converter and then save data in SQL Server database using JDBC Adapter (here we can use RFC as well to save in SAP as well).
I am extending the last blog example to make it simple to understand.

Before we start designing the flow, following steps are mandatory to complete. 

Setup SFTP account In Integration Suite (if not setup, otherwise move to next step)

Note: if you don't have a SFTP account, create one with www.files.com

Create a user credential 

  1. Go to Monitor > Integrations and APIs > and select Security Material (here we manage all our user credentials, certificates etc.)
  2. Create new user credentials (see Create dropdown on your right-hand side)
  3. Type the name you want to give for simplicity I use "SFTPUser" (this will be referred in the flow later on)
  4. Type meaningful description
  5. and choose user credentials from the Type dropdown list
  6. Type your username, password and confirm password for the SFTP account
  7. and select deploy

Get Host Key

  1. Go to Monitor > Integrations and APIs >> Test Connectivity and select SSH tab
  2. Type the SFTP URL (in my case its home456.files.com)
  3. Choose authentication method, client credentials 
  4. Select the newly created user credential from the dropdown list (in my case its SFTPUser)
  5.  Select None for host key verification
  6. If, you wish to check director is accessible or not checked the checkbox and type folder name in the below textbox.
  7. and click Send
  8. If the connection is establish successfully you will get successful message and host key as well copy the host key and save it on notepad for next step.
  9. Then go to Security Material tile once again, and choose "Known Hosts (SSH)" menu-item from upload dropdown (on your right-hand side).
  10. Browse the file you save in step 8 and select deploy.

Create a new Package and iFlow

  1. Go to Design >> select Integrations and APIs
  2. Click on Create, a new window popups, Type package name (I create a package with MIS name)
  3. and select "Save"
  4. Now go to Artifacts tab >> select Integration Flow from "Add" dropdown
  5. Type the name of iFlow (i named it as "Read File from SFTP")
  6. Provide a short description and press "Add and Open in Editor" button
  7. System will redirect you to the editor window 

Create a Source XSD document

  1. use below snippet to create a source xsd document (save it on your local machine). 

 

 

<xs:schema xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema">
    <xs:element name="Employees">
        <xs:complexType>
            <xs:sequence>
                <xs:element name="Employee" maxOccurs="unbounded" minOccurs="1">
                    <xs:complexType>
                        <xs:sequence>
                            <xs:element type="xs:string" name="LastName"/>
                            <xs:element type="xs:string" name="FirstName"/>
                            <xs:element type="xs:string" name="Address"/>
                            <xs:element type="xs:string" name="City"/>
                        </xs:sequence>
                    </xs:complexType>
                </xs:element>
            </xs:sequence>
        </xs:complexType>
    </xs:element>
</xs:schema>

 

 

Create a Destination XSD Document (named it as output.xsd)

 

 

<xs:schema attributeFormDefault="unqualified" elementFormDefault="qualified" xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema">
	<xs:element name="CPI_Request">
		<xs:complexType>
			<xs:sequence>
				<xs:element name="StatementName" maxOccurs="unbounded" minOccurs="1">
					<xs:complexType>
						<xs:sequence>
							<xs:element name="storedProcedureName">
								<xs:complexType>
									<xs:sequence>
										<xs:element type="xs:string" name="table"/>
										<xs:element  name="LastName">
											<xs:complexType>
												<xs:simpleContent>
													<xs:extension base="xs:string">
														<xs:attribute type="xs:string" name="type"/>
													</xs:extension>
												</xs:simpleContent>
											</xs:complexType>
										</xs:element>
										<xs:element  name="FirstName">
											<xs:complexType>
												<xs:simpleContent>
													<xs:extension base="xs:string">
														<xs:attribute type="xs:string" name="type"/>
													</xs:extension>
												</xs:simpleContent>
											</xs:complexType>
										</xs:element>
										<xs:element  name="Address">
											<xs:complexType>
												<xs:simpleContent>
													<xs:extension base="xs:string">
														<xs:attribute type="xs:string" name="type"/>
													</xs:extension>
												</xs:simpleContent>
											</xs:complexType>
										</xs:element>
										<xs:element  name="City">
											<xs:complexType>
												<xs:simpleContent>
													<xs:extension base="xs:string">
														<xs:attribute type="xs:string" name="type"/>
													</xs:extension>
												</xs:simpleContent>
											</xs:complexType>
										</xs:element>
									</xs:sequence>
									<xs:attribute type="xs:string" name="action"/>
								</xs:complexType>
							</xs:element>
						</xs:sequence>
					</xs:complexType>
				</xs:element>
			</xs:sequence>
		</xs:complexType>
	</xs:element>
</xs:schema>

 

 

Upload XSD resources for data conversion (CSV to XML) and Message Mapping

  1. Open the newly created iFlow in edit mode and go to Reference section.
  2. Click "Add" and select "Schema" and then select "XSD".
  3. A new window will open and browse the location where you save both xsd files.
  4. choose both together and press "Add" button.

let's begin

Connect "Sender" with "Start event" and choose SFTP from the Adapter list

SFTP Configuration: open the window add information as per below

Source Tab

FILE ACCESS PARAMETERS 
DirectoryInput (here you type the folder name which content you will be reading in next step)
Filenametest.csv
CONNECTION PARAMETERS 
Addresshome456.files.com(here you have to type your SFTP Location)
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationUser Name/Password
Credential NameSFTPUser (this is the same name you have used while creating user credentials in the step 3)

 

Processing Tab (you can keep by default setting or change as per your requirement)

Post-ProcessingMove File
Archive Directory.archive

So what happens with this setting, once the iFlow reads the file successfully. it moves to .archive folder to avoid duplication of record. 

last step is scheduling the iFlow execution, for this example I configure the scheduler on recur basis every 10 minutes between 11 am till 12am. 

Then go to message transformation and drill down converters

there you find "CSV to XML Converter"

 

Add it, in the design after "Start Event". 

Open the setting pane and rename the control as "Convert CSV Data into XML". After that go to Processing Tab.

  1. choose the source.xsd file from the reference
  2. Add Path to target element in my case its "Employees/Employee"
  3. select the field separator, in my case its "comma"

After that, add Message Mapping (from mapping options).  

Create a new mapping document, name the file as you like i named it as "payload_req". Once the mapping document open, select source and destination XSD files.

Mapped each field as per the image above. Click ok to save the mapping.  Also, remain the control as "Map Data as per JDBC Format". 

@actionEXECUTE
tableInsertPerson (stored procedure name)
@typeVARCHAR

Now, connect "End Event" with Receiver and choose JDBC from the adapter type list. (how to setup JDBC Adapter you can follow this article). 

Open the setting pane, and go to connection tab. here type your JDBC Data Source Alias, in my case its "EmployeeTest".

Now, Save the flow and deploy it. 

 

before iFlow reads the file 

After iFlow read the file

entry.title: Upload Plan data using a ‘Starter Button’ in SAC Story from a File (Excel/CSV)
entry.content:
Introduction: File upload to Planning Model directly without using input forms in SAC Planning by the Planners (End Users). This is a new feature introduced in SAP Analytics Cloud for planning  as part of the Q3’2024 release

This will make planners job easy by simply uploading the file which is prepared in an Excel or CSV format. Prerequisites are the fixed template to be made by SAC Modelers/Administrators as per the Planning Model dimensions.

Current State:

Planning users can only do manual input of data in planning stories.

Future State:

Planning users can upload a file (Planned Data) directly without manual input.

Below is the step-by-step guide to implement Upload functionality in SAC Planning in Stories.

Step 1: Prepare Template file for the upload

First, we need to prepare the Template file.

Step 2: Create Upload Job

Modelers can create an upload job using below option.

From the ‘Data management’ workspace, we can see a new option called ‘Upload Jobs’. This is the new feature added part of Q3’2024 release.

Earlier we had only two options 1. Import Jobs, 2. Export Jobs. from the Data management workspace. Now the 3rd option is ‘Upload Jobs’ is introduced.

Click on ‘Upload Data’ button from Upload Jobs section

Give the New name for the Job and choose your source template file.

 

Step 3: Set up Upload

Once the source template is chosen, you need to ‘Set up Upload’ from the Job created.

Modelers need to create the respective transformations, mapping for the fields as per the planning model.

 

We need to validate and click finish once all the mapping and transformations are ok.

Then click ‘Create’ to complete.

Step 4: Addition of Upload file button to the Story

Provide all the required details in ‘Builder’ Section

Example below:

Label: Demo_Upload

Description: Demo Upload File

Model: Select the Planning Model, where the data gets uploaded

Upload Job: Select the new created Job name from the drop-down menu

Publish: Select required option based on user preferences

Parameters: You can set the default version or from the available options as below

Once the Upload button is added to the Story, user will be able view the widget in SAC story.

Upon clicking the Upload file button from the story, planning user will be prompted to select the version and source file.

Click ‘Run’

Once the file is uploaded, user will be able to see the message that job is completed.

If there are rejection of records, users can download and correct them. Reasons could be below

  1. New master data coming in the file, which was not available in dimensions
  2. File format issue, which is not matching with the pre-defined template

Modelers can see the job status from the ‘Data Management’ workspace under ‘Upload Jobs’

 

Summary:

Upload data using Starter button is helpful when users are preparing the plan data in an offline excel/csv file or for mass data flat files. This is user friendly functionality provided in recent releases for SAC Planning. Users will be able to use both manual inputs and flat file uploads together.

entry.title: Downtime optimization using DoDMO during ECC to S/4 Conversion Run
entry.content:
In this blog, I will talk about the downtime optimization using 

1. DMOVE2S4

2. Downtime Optimized DMO (DoDMO)

Introduction: In today's scenario of digital transformation when all SAP customers are adopting the hyperscalers on cloud, converting there ECC system to S/4HANA. The major challenges during the transformation journey is business downtime.

When we talk about very big source database size eg. - 25-30 TB then it will be more difficult to manage the downtime. Here I will talk about the downtime optimization using downtime optimized DMO run.

Scenario Description - The source database size was ~30 TB and running on SQL Server that we need to migrate on SAP HANA database running on RISE environment. We need to finish the conversion activities along with technical downtime within conversion weekend considering less business disruption.

Taking into consideration of large database size, we finalized to perform the migration using - Classical RUN (DMOVE2S4) followed by downtime optimized DMO(DoDMO).

Solution Description: In order to achieve the downtime expectation, we planned our first conversion cycle using DMOVE2S4 method to capture the following -

  • Uptime Table Selection for DoDMO 
  • Impact Analysis for DoDMO
  • Downtime baseline with classical run

DMOVE2S4 and DoDMO : This functionality was released with SUM2.0SP17 onwards, using this you can convert/move anyDB to S/4HANA in one step. SUM does not give name of this feature in dialog/user interface.

We need to install the additional application server for source on migration VM based on ECS cloud environment. Also, SUM will provide the option to move your ASCS instance on the migration server -

Since the migration is happening over network so Latency should be lower than 20ms and bandwidth needs to higher then 400mbps. You can plan to use benchmarking tool for testing the latency and transfer rate. After completing the first classical run, our downtime execution phase took ~90+ hrs which didn't met the downtime expectation -

Now we need to plan for next cycle using downtime-optimized DMO(DoDMO) to reduce the technical downtime. So, we installed the SUM toolbox in source environment (Ref. KBA - 3092738 ) for uptime table selection and Impact Analysis for DoDMO (Ref. KBA 2547309). 

We need to consider multiple factors before finalizing table list like - Table export duration in classical run, Table Change Rate, Source Database Size, etc.). Also, need to exclude tables due to technical restriction suggested by SUM TOOLBOX and SAP KBA 2547309

Now we have table list ready so select the following option and supply the list to SUM -

Since this method is working on record and replay method so uptime changes by end user are considered and delta will be recorded. This recorded data will be replayed followed by downtime table migration during execution phase. The uptime migration of BASIS table will happen in EU_CLONE_CP2STAB_RUN Phase and other tables will be migrated during EU_CLONE_MIG_OPTDMOP_INI_RUN phase. 

SUM triggers two replication process types:
                 R3load: used to replicate most tables
                 ABAP work processes: used for tables requiring collision handling (tables that get an import)

You can monitor the replication status in CRR control center -

Based on the uptime table selection and data footprint taken, we were able to migrate our 65-70% data during uptime migration. Below are the runtime of major SUM phases for DoDMO run -

So finally, we were able to save 72% of downtime compared to DMOVE2S4 classical run and able to reduce technical downtime from 94.5 hrs to 26.5 hrs for large database size of ~30 TB which is promising results to manage weekend downtime window -

You can consider following Sources to optimize your migration further -

  • Upgrade Guide: Conversion to SAP S/4HANA or SAP BW/4HANA using SUM: ABAP Systems on XXX
  • 2547309 - Downtime-optimized DMO with SUM 2.0 
  • 1616401 - Parallelism in the Upgrades, EhPs and Support Packages implementations 
  • 2186744 - FAQ: SAP HANA Parameters 
  • 2597701 - Performance analysis for EU_CLONE_MIG_DT_RUN phase 
  • 2383750 - DMO: How to speed up the export of cluster tables 

Hope this blog will help many customers to plan their migration journey from On Premise to ECS Private cloud network using DoDMO and DMOVE2S4 procedure.

If this blog seems to be useful to you then please don't forget to put your comments

Happy Reading!!!

Regards,

Vinay Shrimali

entry.title: Understanding Replication Flow Results in Azure (ADLS Gen2)
entry.content:
Introduction:

By the end of this document, you would be familiar with the output of the replication flow that is ingested to Azure (ADLS Gen2)

Prerequisites:

Artifact - Datasphere Replication Flow

Source - Any Source (in this blog considered S/4 as the source)

Target - Azure Data Lake Storage Gen 2 (ADLS Gen2)

Steps:

  • Create a replication flow and target should be azure.

  • To know how to create a target with Azure ADLS gen 2 please refer to the blog Datasphere to ADLS Gen2 Connectivity - SAP Community
  • In the replication flow, based on the selection of load type, the folder structure is created
  • Load Type - Initial Only
  • Load Type - Initial and Delta
  • Folder Structure 
    • .sap.partfile.metadata : 

      This file includes the details of table fields and their data types. Additionally, it provides the configuration selected for the target. It will also be present in both the initial and delta folders, with identical and unchanging contents. It might change when SAP releases the Schema evolution feature. (i.e any change in the table structure would be automatically picked like SLT)

       

    • Initial Folder
      • _SUCCESS file:

        This is an empty file that is generated as an indication of completion of initial load.

      • .sap.partfile.metadata :

        As mentioned earlier, it will have the field level details

        The files starting with ‘Part-’ contain the data. Their naming convention follows the format ‘Part-<GUID>’, which we cannot control. The initial load may consist of multiple files, depending on the number of records in the source table. The first file has a GUID starting with ‘A’, the second with ‘T’, and the third with ‘Z’, indicating a logic of file generation with GUIDs in ascending order.

    • Delta Folder:

      Contains .sap.partfile.metadata file and delta files. Files will be generated if there is delta data fetched for that run depending on the frequency.

      Why Should Truncate option be checked during the reload?

      Whenever a load is triggered, after the initial load is completed, _SUCCESS and .sap.partfile.metadata files are generated in the initial folder. The _SUCCESS file indicates the completion of the initial load. When a reload is triggered, these two files are expected to be generated again. If the truncate option is not selected, this will cause an error since the folders already contain these files.

entry.title: Re: Goods issue with wrong unit cost.
entry.content:
In SAP Business One (SAP B1), when dealing with incorrect item costs due to foreign exchange rate issues, and with quantities being correct, you need to address both the financial impact and the accuracy of the inventory values. Here’s Spotify Pie a structured approach to resolving the problem:

1. Identify the Impact
First, understand the extent of the impact:

Incorrect Costs: Determine how the wrong unit costs have affected the general ledger (GL) and inventory valuation.
Correct Exchange Rates: Confirm that the foreign currency exchange rates have been updated correctly.
2. Correcting Inventory Values
There are two main approaches to correct the values: inventory revaluation and reversal entries. Given that quantities are correct, you should focus on addressing the cost discrepancies:

Option 1: Inventory Revaluation
Revaluate Inventory: You can use the Inventory Revaluation feature in SAP B1 to adjust the item costs. This method adjusts the valuation of inventory items based on the new correct unit costs.

Steps:

Navigate to Inventory -> Inventory Revaluation.
Select the relevant items and enter the new unit costs.
Post the revaluation to update the inventory valuation and correct the GL accounts.
Considerations:

Ensure that you revalue only those items where the cost discrepancy exists.
This approach is ideal if the cost discrepancies are significant and widespread.
Option 2: Reversal Entries
Reverse Incorrect Transactions:
Goods Receipt (GR): If the GR documents were posted with the wrong cost, you may need to reverse or cancel those GR entries and repost them with the correct costs.

Goods Issue (GI): Similarly, for GI transactions, reverse the incorrect entries if possible, and then repost them with the correct costs.

Steps for Reversal:

Identify the incorrect GR and GI transactions.
Reverse or cancel these transactions in SAP B1.
Repost the GR and GI transactions with the correct unit costs.
Considerations:

This method might be more time-consuming, especially if there are many transactions.
It’s crucial to ensure that reversing transactions do not affect the accuracy of quantities.
3. Update GL Accounts
After making corrections to inventory costs, verify that the GL accounts are updated accordingly. Ensure that the adjustments to inventory values reflect correctly in the financial statements.

entry.title: Re: ATP check for sto purchase orders
entry.content: In the mentioned SSCUI you can find the so-called checking rule (e. g. 'A'). This means that the Checking Group and Checking Rule together define the Scope of Check like in your screenshot. In your screenshot next to 'A' the label is 'Sales Order'. However, when using 'A' in STO then one can state that STO and Sales Order behave the same from Scope of Check perspective. There is no dedicated Checking Rule for STO such that the label would state 'STO'. Maybe this is the confusion. If not, please feel free to come back 🙂
